name: BullMQ
what_it_does: Redis-based job and message queue for Node.js/TypeScript with priority queues, rate limiting, job scheduling, and parent-child job dependencies. BullMQ Pro adds additional features including batching and observability.
use_cases: Dispatching background jobs from Node.js agents with retry and priority control
Rate-limited API call queuing to stay within provider limits
Scheduled and delayed job execution from automated workflows
Fan-out patterns with parent-child job dependencies and flow producers
Processing AI pipeline steps as ordered, retriable job sequences
not_for: Non-Node.js environments (official SDK is Node.js only; use Celery for Python)
Long-running durable workflows requiring persistent state beyond Redis TTL
Very high-throughput messaging at Kafka scale (Redis is the bottleneck)
best_when: A Node.js/TypeScript agent needs reliable background job processing with priorities, rate limiting, and retry logic backed by Redis.
avoid_when: You need cross-language workers, durable multi-step saga patterns, or managed infrastructure without Redis.

auth.notes: No built-in auth API. Authentication is delegated entirely to Redis connection credentials. No API key, token, or HTTP interface. Access control is at the Redis/network layer.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Fully free and open source (MIT). Infrastructure costs (Redis) are separate.
pricing.paid_tiers: BullMQ Pro $259/mo for additional features (batching, groups, observability integrations).
pricing.notes: OSS BullMQ covers most production use cases at no cost. BullMQ Pro adds enterprise features. Redis infrastructure is the primary operational cost. Bull (the predecessor) is deprecated in favor of BullMQ.

performance.rate_limits: No inherent rate limits; throughput depends on Redis capacity and worker count

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Failed jobs include the JavaScript error object with stack trace stored in Redis. Worker-level errors are emitted as events. Error serialization to/from Redis can lose non-serializable error properties. Bull Board UI available for visual inspection.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Jobs can be given explicit IDs — adding a job with a duplicate ID is a no-op in the default deduplication mode. Agents should generate deterministic job IDs for idempotent dispatch. No built-in exactly-once processing guarantee.
known_agent_gotchas: No REST API — agents must be Node.js applications or use a custom REST wrapper
Redis memory usage grows if completed/failed jobs are not periodically pruned — configure removeOnComplete and removeOnFail
Stalled job detection requires correct lockDuration — too short causes false-positive stalled detection and duplicate processing
Workers must explicitly handle SIGTERM for graceful shutdown — unhandled shutdown leaves jobs in active state
Parent-child job dependencies require Flow Producer — not all job relationship patterns are supported
Rate limiter uses Redis time — clock skew between workers and Redis can cause unexpected rate limit behavior
Bull (v3) and BullMQ (v4+) are incompatible — do not mix clients against the same Redis queue

score_rationale: BullMQ is the leading job queue for Node.js with a well-designed API, explicit job IDs for partial idempotency, and good documentation. fq_score reflects solid TypeScript support and feature richness for Node.js contexts. af_score is significantly penalized by the Node.js-only SDK with no REST API, making it inaccessible to polyglot or externally-orchestrated agent systems. Redis dependency adds operational overhead. Well-suited for agents embedded in Node.js services.

security_score_components.security_notes: Redis-based queue — Redis auth is the security boundary. Use Redis AUTH and TLS in production. Job data stored in Redis — encrypt sensitive payloads.
